One of the students in a class I teach, a little older student than most, once told me a story that initially provided a great example of just the reciprocity principle, but which eventually proved to be an amazing example of the power of the unity principle. After hearing one of my lectures about the power of reciprocity and how it could influence people to reply, in one instance, to Christmas cards that had been sent by a complete stranger, the student reported that she was engaged in just such an exchange. Ten years prior, she had received such a card from the Harrison's of Santa Barbara, People she was confident she didn't know. Baffled but motivated by the power of reciprocity, the student and her family repaid the gesture by sending this set of complete strangers a card of their own before the holiday season closed. By virtue of the rule of reciprocation, it just seemed like the right thing to do. The next year, the Harrison sent them another card. And of course, my student's family again reciprocated. Soon, it was an annual tradition, and the two families exchanged Christmas cards and newsletters for many years. I thought I recognized this charming story as an example of reciprocity, but months later, this student returned to my office to tell me that the story had taken an interesting turn, one that could not be explained by simple reciprocity. The student's son Skip was getting ready to head off to college in Santa Barbara, California. And due to some work being done on his dorm building, Skip's room wasn't yet ready. Rather than having him stay in a hotel, Skip's parents thought to themselves, who do we know in Santa Barbara? The Harrison's, of course, the Harrison's. So they reached out to the Harrison family and asked if Skip could stay with them for a few days. Sure, the Harrison's replied, we would love to have Skip stay with us. And so he did. Now, while reciprocity could explain the exchange of Christmas cards, it couldn't explain why the Harrison's would agree to share their home for several days with a college student they'd never met. After all, the Harrison's owed no debt to Skip's family. Cards and newsletters had been exchanged equally at that point. Getting more than simple reciprocity was at work. That thing, I realized, was the principle of unity which we will cover next.
